front: Site: name is Shona for "House of Stone"; founded by ancestors of the Shona in the 11th century, and the Torwa Dynasty at Khami prospered after this people abandoned the city
Site: located near Harare and near Masvingo, and shares its name with the modern country whose capital is Harare
Event: racist colonial Rhodesian leaders denied that the Shona had built this drystone city; it later became a political symbol for Black activists opposed to Prime Minister Ian Smith
Artifact: one of this site's eight soapstone bird carvings appears on the modern flag of Zimbabwe, and formerly inspired a national symbol on Rhodesian currency
Structure: sometimes called an "elliptical temple," this structure's double walls (running about 820 feet) enclose the Conical Tower
Structure: a roughly 30-foot (5.5-meter) tower built between the two walls of the Great Enclosure
Structure: contains a temple and the Eastern Enclosure, which originally held the site's soapstone bird sculptures atop monoliths before they were moved in the 1880s
Structure: this part of the site contains numerous dwellings made of daga, a mixture of mud and thatch
Concept: pits used by the site's inhabitants to store massive amounts of water, per 2022 research
Figure: archaeologist who showed the site's walls progressed from simpler to more complex styles over time (rather than deteriorating), and first suggested a medieval African dating for the city
Artifact: eight of these soapstone sculptures were recovered from the site, originally set atop monoliths in the Hill Complex's Eastern Enclosure
work: The Zimbabwe Birds and Material Culture

front: Figure: hacked the base off one of this site's soapstone bird carvings and sold it to Cecil Rhodes
work: The Zimbabwe Birds and Material Culture

front: Concept: one interpretation holds that the Zimbabwe Birds depict these spirit-beings, believed to use lightning to "stitch together heaven and earth"
work: The Zimbabwe Birds and Material Culture

front: Figure: archaeologist who devised a taxonomy of the Zimbabwe Birds based on whether they depict straight or squatting legs
work: The Zimbabwe Birds and Material Culture

front: Figure: historian who noted that this site's copper ingots were simpler than the "St. Andrew's crosses" ingots found at the nearby "sleeping cow" site (Ingombe Ilede)
work: The Zimbabwe Birds and Material Culture

front: Concept: spirit mediums at the site were believed to channel these two types of ancestor spirits
Title: the kings who ruled from this site bore this title

Figure: archaeologist who argued in "Where You Are the Girls Gather to Play" that decorative motifs at the site link to gendered dice symbols, and that a structure with male and female entrances served as a girls' initiation school

Event: late in the site's history, a civil war between Changamire and Mutota rocked the polity ruled from it, before it was taken over by this empire, whose Shona rulers built imitation cities elsewhere
Figure: pioneering female anthropologist who faced significant hostility for her assertions (of African, not foreign, origin) about this site
Historian: controversially claimed the site was built by a Jewish-descended group called the Lemba
Institution: Swahili Coast sultanate that received/traded gold with this site
Site: older southern African site on the Limpopo River, once theorized to have also built Great Zimbabwe to its north; may have been founded by the same early Shona people
Event: building at Great Zimbabwe stopped in this century, before Western archaeologists later linked it to the biblical land of Ophir
Site: after Great Zimbabwe's decline, its stoneworking craftsmanship moved south to this city, later capital of the gold-trading Torwa Dynasty
Figure: according to legend, this man founded the Mutapa Empire after leaving Great Zimbabwe with his followers to find new sources of salt
Figure: hunter/trader who first sighted Great Zimbabwe for Europeans; his companion, German explorer Karl Mauch, linked the city to the biblical Queen of Sheba and the land of Ophir
Figure: captain of the Portuguese garrison at Sofala who made the earliest written record of Great Zimbabwe
